pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-24

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Treat the long tail (e.g., 90th percentile wait time) primarily as an ownership/routing issue: complex cross-service changes are waiting on a small set of senior reviewers, even after reviewer rotation improved the median.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 states median wait improved from 19 to 13 hours after reviewer rotation, the 90th percentile remains 46 hours because complex changes wait for the same two senior reviewers, and recommends treating tail latency as an ownership-routing problem. It also distinguishes routine from cross-service/shared-service changes.

claimJudgments.claimText: Introduce a fast-track path limited to documentation and configuration changes when tests are healthy, while keeping required approvals intact and explicitly disallowing automation from bypassing branch protection.
claimJudgments.explanation: S6 says fast-track labels are approved for documentation and configuration changes when tests are healthy, automation may organize/suggest but must not bypass required approvals, and branch protection remains in force.

claimJudgments.claimText: Harden cross-service review requirements by ensuring an owning-team reviewer is required for cross-service changes, since incidents were linked to fast-tracking cross-service work without the correct owning team.
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 links two incidents to fast-tracking cross-service changes without the owning team and calls for different treatment of cross-boundary changes. S6 says cross-service changes still require an owning-team reviewer even if predicted low risk.

claimJudgments.claimText: Use a queue helper to reduce reviewer coordination overhead—suggest reviewers, cluster similar low-risk changes, and surface missing/conflicting ownership metadata—rather than relying on bot approvals.
claimJudgments.explanation: S4 supports helper capabilities to suggest reviewers, group/cluster low-risk changes, and flag missing ownership data. S5 supports surfacing missing or conflicting ownership as queue risk. S2 supports skepticism toward automated approvals and preference for assignment/explainability.

claimJudgments.claimText: Add reviewer-visible risk labels that explain why a change is considered low risk (ownership metadata, test health, file categories) and make the label challengeable before merge to build trust without weakening quality controls.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 says reviewers wanted risk labels explaining why a change is safe to fast-track, including ownership metadata, test health, and file categories, and that the fast-track label should be challengeable before merge.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation is well aligned with the packet: it targets tail latency through ownership routing, uses automation for organization rather than approval, limits fast-track to docs/config with healthy tests and preserved approvals, and requires owning-team review for cross-service work. It appropriately avoids claiming that automation can verify semantic ownership or bypass branch protection.

citationSupport.note: S1 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
